splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year, or whether actuaries may select or update assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined by the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the period of supervision does not run while the person is a fugitive.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory authority for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts may not create such a doctrine without explicit congressional authorization.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing successive motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prevents raising any previously presented claim — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255, or whether they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s separate requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law. The answer determines the gatekeeping strictness for federal prisoners seeking successive post-conviction relief.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring any claim previously presented.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar does not apply to federal prisoners filing under § 2255, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to state voting procedures. The question determines whether such disputes must wait until after an election, when they may be moot or create chaos, or whether candidates may challenge election rules prospectively.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that allegedly dilute votes or alter election outcomes, allowing pre-election challenges.

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's speculative and generalized allegations about ballot-counting procedures do not establish a concrete injury sufficient for Article III standing.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity or agency/Sentencing Commission deference takes priority when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions in criminal cases. At least three circuits hold that lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held the opposite. The issue has gained urgency after the Supreme Court's decision in Loper Bright overruling Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, so ambiguity must be resolved in the defendant's favor.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference prevails over the rule of lenity, allowing the Commission's anti-defendant interpretation of ambiguous Guidelines provisions to control.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary (Application Note 1) to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2 may expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy, aiding and abetting, and attempt in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is a valid interpretation of the guideline or impermissibly expands its text. This affects whether defendants with prior inchoate drug convictions are sentenced as career offenders under significantly higher Guidelines ranges.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative and binding interpretation of § 4B1.2(b), and inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as controlled substance offenses for career offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary cannot expand guideline text to include inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its plain meaning, especially after Kisor v. Wilkie.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any single criterion is disqualifying. The split leads to dramatically different sentences for defendants with identical criminal histories depending on where they are prosecuted.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means all three criminal-history criteria must be met before a defendant is disqualified from safety-valve relief below the mandatory minimum.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) may be considered as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' § 924(c) sentences — as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar reliance on non-retroactive legal changes, while others allow them as one factor among many. This affects thousands of inmates serving lengthy stacked sentences.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may not support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state statute covers substances not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled, or whether any state-scheduled substance suffices. The split affects career offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State scheduling suffices — no federal limitation required
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2(b) broadly includes offenses under state law regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled, and there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a controlled substance offense only if the substance at issue is also controlled under federal law, using a categorical approach that compares the state statute to the federal schedules.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have reached the opposite conclusion, creating a split that affects debtors' incentives to convert and the amount available to creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A) and § 541(a), post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and § 348(f)(1)(A)'s purpose establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, not the estate, upon good-faith conversion.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose revocation sentences under 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e) that are designed to punish the defendant for violating conditions of supervised release, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit, under United States v. Lewis, permits district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation, placing it at the extreme end of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that revocation sentences should address the breach of trust in the court's supervision rather than serve as direct punishment for the new conduct.

positions.label: Courts may punish for the violation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose revocation sentences that expressly punish defendants for the conduct constituting the supervised release violation.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions breach of trust, not punishment for new conduct
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should sanction the breach of trust inherent in violating supervised release conditions rather than serve as punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order denying a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment, given that the motion is filed in a criminal case but concerns a civil-type fee claim.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment fee order follows the civil 30-day timeline or the criminal 14-day timeline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, but a minority position applies the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment fee motion arises in a criminal case, the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier,' extends to state negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or selection of motor carriers. The split affects the ability of injured parties to recover from brokers for accidents caused by carriers the brokers selected.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's services in arranging transportation, which falls within the FAAAA's preemption scope.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because the FAAAA's safety exception or the nature of negligence claims takes them outside the preemption clause's scope.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) may be used as a vehicle to address potential constitutional or legal errors in the underlying conviction or sentence — issues that typically must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255. Some circuits allow these factors to be considered, while others hold that § 3582 cannot serve as a substitute for § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider the legal or constitutional infirmity of the underlying conviction or sentence as a factor in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a proper vehicle to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine of 28 U.S.C. § 1291.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denials of motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment. Other circuits have not uniformly recognized immediate appealability for such orders, creating a potential divergence in appellate jurisdiction standards.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is a collateral order because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost if review were deferred to final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denials of motions to seal, including those involving medical information, do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's strict requirements and must await final judgment for appellate review.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments?
splits.description: The circuits have taken different approaches to the standards for establishing liability when business operators discriminate against or unfairly treat customers. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach, indicating a circuit split on the relevant legal questions affecting hospitality, retail, and service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: The Second Circuit applied its own standards for evaluating discrimination claims in hospitality/business establishment contexts, which the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards for evaluating discrimination or unfair treatment claims against business establishments, creating a split that prompted SCOTUS review.
